text:
Back to spending 24/7 at work and sleeping in conference/server/factory rooms. 

I must be super focused on 𝕏/xAI and Tesla (plus Starship launch next week), as we have critical technologies rolling out. 

As evidenced by the 𝕏 uptime issues this week, major operational improvements need to be made. The failover redundancy should have worked, but did not.

text:
For the past several days, Tesla has been testing self-driving Model Y cars (no one in driver’s seat) on Austin public streets with no incidents. 

A month ahead of schedule.

Next month, first self-delivery from factory to customer.
There is no change to tax incentives for oil & gas, just EV/solar

text:
With the version 3 Starlink satellites, which start launching on Starship in 6 to 9 months, we should be able to get latency below 20ms. 

The new, much larger satellites will be at ~350km instead of ~550km altitude, which cuts latency due to speed of light down to ~5ms. 

Also, the Starlink laser links transmit data ~40% faster in vacuum vs fiber, so packets will move faster than anything on the ground. Helps to have physics on your side!

embedded_text:
Rounded to the nearest percent, 100% of Earth’s available energy over the long run is sunlight.

Fission is the only thing that even comes close, and it still rounds to zero.

Solar is paramount for the prosperity of future humans.

(and present ones)

text:
80% efficiency is impossible. What magical photon frequencies and electron energy levels is he talking about?

Even triple-junction gallium arsenide, which costs crazy money, is only ~40%.

Also, there are major energy conversion losses converting PV electricity to photons to transmit to Earth and then converting those photons back to moving electrons.

Sigh.

text:
Starship made it to the scheduled ship engine cutoff, so big improvement over last flight! Also, no significant loss of heat shield tiles during ascent.

Leaks caused loss of main tank pressure during the coast and re-entry phase. Lot of good data to review.

Launch cadence for next 3 flights will be faster, at approximately 1 every 3 to 4 weeks.

text:
The angle of attack is significantly different during ascent, hypersonic flight and subsonic flight, so it is hard to overlap tiles consistent with the direction of air flow. 

Also, super hot plasma water-falling over the height differences between overlapping tiles would create heat concentrations that erode the tiles.

raw: {"__typename": "Tweet", "card": {"legacy": {"binding_values": [{"key": "unified_card", "value": {"string_value": "{\"component_objects\":{\"details_1\":{\"type\":\"grok_share\",\"data\":{\"profile_user\":{\"id_str\":\"44196397\",\"profile_image_url_https\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/2035314704307081216\\/71U1ftM3.jpg\",\"name\":\"Elon Musk\",\"screen_name\":\"elonmusk\",\"highlighted_label\":{\"description\":\"X\",\"badge\":{\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1955359038532653056\\/OSHY3ewP_bigger.jpg\"},\"url\":{\"urlType\":\"DeepLink\",\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/twitter.com\\/X\"},\"userLabelType\":\"BusinessLabel\",\"userLabelDisplayType\":\"Badge\"},\"verified\":false,\"is_blue_verified\":true},\"grok_user\":{\"id_str\":\"1720665183188922368\",\"profile_image_url_https\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1893219113717342208\\/Vgg2hEPa.jpg\",\"name\":\"Grok\",\"screen_name\":\"grok\",\"highlighted_label\":{\"description\":\"xAI\",\"badge\":{\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/pbs.twimg.com\\/profile_images\\/1769430779845611520\\/lIgjSJGU_bigger.jpg\"},\"url\":{\"urlType\":\"DeepLink\",\"url\":\"https:\\/\\/twitter.com\\/xai\"},\"userLabelType\":\"BusinessLabel\",\"userLabelDisplayType\":\"Badge\"},\"verified\":false,\"is_blue_verified\":true,\"verified_type\":\"Business\"},\"id\":\"OtzOpgd7evRqfx0sV230L5SeU\",\"conversation_preview\":[{\"message\":\"Think harder\",\"sender\":\"USER\",\"grokMode\":\"NORMAL\",\"mediaUrls\":[]},{\"message\":\"Let\\u2019s dive deeper into the complexities of Elon Musk\\u2019s response in the target post (https:\\/\\/x.com\\/elonmusk\\/status\\/1927273046370378240) and unpack the engineering and physics challenges SpaceX faces with the Starship heat shield, as well as the broader context from the thread and related web results. I\\u2019ll analyze the problem from multiple angles, considering the constraints Musk highlights, the suggestions from other users, and the historical and technical background of heat shields in spaceflight.\\n--\\nUnderstanding Musk\\u2019s Core Points in the Target Post\\nMusk\\u2019s response addresses Judge Gibson\\u2019s suggestion to install heat shield tiles like \\\"fish scales or shingles on a roof\\\" instead of the current flat, bathroom-floor-like arrangement of hexagonal tiles on Starship. Musk outlines two primary reasons why this overlapping approach is problematic:\\na. Varying Angles of Attack Across Flight Phases\\nWhat Musk Says: \\\"The angle of attack is significantly different during ascent, hypersonic flight, and subsonic flight, so it is hard to overlap tiles consistent with the direction of airflow.\\\"\\nAnalysis: \\n  - Angle of Attack (AoA): This refers to the angle between the vehicle\\u2019s longitudinal axis and the direction of the oncoming airflow. During ascent, Starship is launched vertically, with an AoA close to zero as it aligns with its upward trajectory through the atmosphere. In hypersonic reentry, Starship reenters belly-first at a high AoA (around 40-60 degrees) to maximize drag and slow down while exposing the heat shield to the most intense plasma flow. During subsonic flight, as it approaches landing, the AoA changes again, likely closer to zero as it maneuvers for a vertical landing.\\n  - Impact on Overlapping Tiles: Overlapping tiles (like shingles) are designed to shed airflow in a specific direction, typically aligned with the flow to prevent turbulence or separation. However, with Starship\\u2019s AoA changing dramatically across these phases, the airflow direction shifts relative to the tiles. Tiles overlapped for one phase (e.g., reentry) might create drag or turbulence in another (e.g., ascent), potentially leading to aerodynamic instability or increased stress on the tiles. For example:\\n    - During ascent, upward airflow might catch on the edges of overlapping tiles, causing lift-off forces that could dislodge them.\\n    - During reentry, the high AoA means plasma flow is not parallel to the surface but at an angle, which could exacerbate the \\\"waterfall\\\" effect Musk describes next.\\n  - Engineering Challenge: Designing an overlapping tile system that accommodates these varying AoA conditions would require a dynamic or adaptive tile structure, which adds complexity, weight, and potential failure points\\u2014counter to SpaceX\\u2019s goal of a simple, reusable heat shield.\\nb. Plasma \\\"Waterfall\\\" Effect and Heat Concentrations\\nWhat Musk Says: \\\"Super hot plasma water-falling over the height differences between overlapping tiles would create heat concentrations that erode the tiles.\\\"\\nAnalysis:\\n  - Plasma During Reentry: As noted in the Physics Stack Exchange web result, during reentry, the shockwave ahead of Starship compresses and heats the atmosphere to extreme temperatures\\u2014up to 68,000\\u00b0C in the shock zone\\u2014turning it into plasma (ionized gas). This plasma flows over the vehicle, transferring heat to the heat shield. While the spacecraft itself doesn\\u2019t reach these temperatures (thanks to the heat shield), the tiles still face temperatures around 1,377\\u00b0C (2,510\\u00b0F), as SpaceX\\u2019s 2019 tests showed (Space.com, 2019).\\n  - Waterfall Effect: Musk uses the metaphor of \\\"water-falling\\\" to describe how plasma behaves like a fluid. In an overlapping tile design, each tile would be slightly raised above the one below it, creating a step or height difference. As plasma flows over these steps, it would:\\n    - Stagnate or Turbulate: The step causes the flow to slow down or separate, creating localized areas of high pressure and temperature (heat concentrations).\\n    - Erode Tiles: These heat concentrations would increase thermal stress on the tile edges, potentially leading to ablation (material loss) or cracking. The Space Shuttle\\u2019s tiles, for instance, often experienced erosion and required replacement after each flight due to similar plasma interactions (Encyclopedia.com).\\n  - Comparison to Current Design: SpaceX\\u2019s hexagonal tiles are laid flat and flush with minimal gaps, which Musk noted in 2019 as a benefit: \\u201cno straight path for hot gas to accelerate through the gaps\\u201d (Space.com, 2019). Overlapping tiles introduce more pronounced height differences, amplifying the risk of plasma-induced erosion compared to the current design.\\n--\\nBroader Context: Why Is This So Hard?\\nMusk\\u2019s response reflects a deeper challenge in spaceflight: creating a truly reusable heat shield for orbital reentry, especially under the extreme conditions of Mars entry with a heavy payload.\\na. Historical Precedent\\nSpace Shuttle Example: Musk notes that \\u201cno one has ever made a truly reusable orbital heat shield,\\u201d citing the Space Shuttle\\u2019s tiles, which required extensive rework between flights. The Shuttle used ceramic tiles (HRSI and LRSI) that dissipated heat efficiently but were fragile\\u2014several tiles were often shaken loose or damaged by plasma during reentry, necessitating costly repairs (Encyclopedia.com).\\nStarship\\u2019s Ambition: Unlike the Shuttle, which operated in low Earth orbit (LEO), Starship is designed for deep-space missions to Mars. Mars\\u2019 atmosphere is thinner (about 1% of Earth\\u2019s density at sea level), but entry speeds are still high (around 5-7 km\\/s for Mars vs. 7.8 km\\/s for Earth), and the payload mass increases the ballistic coefficient (mass-to-area ratio), meaning more heat load per unit area. This makes the heat shield problem even tougher.\\nb. Ballistic Coefficient and Mars Entry\\nMusk\\u2019s Earlier Reply (Post 1927206115533549639): Musk explains that Mars entry with a heavy payload requires a high ballistic coefficient, making stainless steel alone \\u201chopeless\\u201d for surviving reentry without a heat shield.\\nPhysics Insight:\\n  - Ballistic Coefficient (\\u03b2): Defined as \\\\( \\\\beta = \\\\frac\\u007bm\\u007d\\u007bC_d A\\u007d \\\\), where \\\\( m \\\\) is mass, \\\\( C_d \\\\) is the drag coefficient, and \\\\( A \\\\) is the cross-sectional area. A higher \\u03b2 means the vehicle decelerates less in the atmosphere, experiencing a longer, more intense heat pulse during reentry.\\n  - Mars vs. Earth: On Earth, Starship\\u2019s belly-first reentry maximizes \\\\( A \\\\), lowering \\u03b2 and spreading the heat load. On Mars, the thinner atmosphere provides less drag, so even with a large \\\\( A \\\\), the vehicle retains more velocity, increasing the heat flux. A heavy payload (higher \\\\( m \\\\)) further increases \\u03b2, exacerbating the problem.\\n  - Stainless Steel Limitations: John Carmack\\u2019s suggestion (Post 1927127025837326700) of a \\u201cfluffier\\u201d stainless steel structure (lower \\u03b2) might work for Earth reentry, but Musk counters that Mars entry demands a robust heat shield. Stainless steel melts at around 1,500\\u00b0C, far below the 1,377\\u00b0C the tiles already endure, let alone the plasma\\u2019s peak temperatures.\\nc. SpaceX\\u2019s Supply Chain Challenge\\nMusk mentions building an \\u201cenormously shocking\\u201d heat shield factory at the Cape, highlighting the scale of the problem. SpaceX had to develop the entire supply chain\\u2014from raw materials (glass, alumina) to finished tiles\\u2014because no existing solution met their needs for reusability, durability, and mass production. This underscores the novelty and difficulty of the task.\\n--\\nEvaluating Alternative Suggestions from the Thread\\nOther users in the thread propose creative solutions to the heat shield problem. Let\\u2019s assess their feasibility in light of Musk\\u2019s constraints.\\na. Shark Denticles (Post 1927279196029509937 by RationalEtienne)\\nSuggestion: Use a heat shield inspired by shark denticles\\u2014small, interlocking, non-overlapping ceramic elements with microchannels to guide plasma flow smoothly, reducing turbulence and heat concentrations.\\nAnalysis:\\n  - Pros: Shark denticles reduce drag and turbulence in water by channeling flow through microgrooves. A similar design could, in theory, streamline plasma flow over Starship, minimizing hotspots. The non-overlapping aspect avoids the \\u201cwaterfall\\u201d issue Musk describes.\\n  - Cons:\\n    - Material Strength: Shark denticles are biological structures, not designed for 1,377\\u00b0C plasma. Ceramic equivalents would need to withstand thermal shock, mechanical stress, and repeated reentries without degrading.\\n    - Microchannels and Plasma: Plasma at hypersonic speeds is highly turbulent and ionized, unlike water. Microchannels might clog with ablated material or create unintended pressure gradients, leading to localized heating.\\n    - Complexity: Manufacturing millions of microchanneled ceramic elements and ensuring they interlock perfectly across Starship\\u2019s surface would be a production nightmare, increasing costs and failure risks.\\n  - Feasibility: Promising in concept, but the practical challenges of scaling this bio-inspired design to a spacecraft under extreme conditions make it a long shot without significant R&D.\\nb. Magnetic Field to Repel Plasma (Post 1927352323644223769 by TeslaPI)\\nSuggestion: Use a magnetic field to repel plasma, similar to fusion reactors.\\nAnalysis:\\n  - Physics Basis: Plasma is ionized gas, meaning it\\u2019s charged and can be influenced by magnetic fields. In fusion reactors (e.g., tokamaks), magnetic fields confine plasma to prevent it from touching the reactor walls.\\n  - Application to Starship:\\n    - Magnetic Field Strength: To deflect plasma at reentry speeds (7.8 km\\/s on Earth, 5-7 km\\/s on Mars), the magnetic field would need to be extraordinarily strong. The Lorentz force on a charged particle is \\\\( F = q(v \\\\times B) \\\\), where \\\\( q \\\\) is the charge, \\\\( v \\\\) is velocity, and \\\\( B \\\\) is the magnetic field. Given the high \\\\( v \\\\), \\\\( B \\\\) would need to be on the order of tens or hundreds of Tesla\\u2014far beyond what current superconductors can achieve on a spacecraft.\\n    - Power Requirements: Generating such a field requires massive electrical power, which Starship doesn\\u2019t have during reentry (it\\u2019s unpowered, relying on aerodynamic control).\\n    - Weight and Complexity: Superconducting magnets are heavy and require cryogenic cooling, adding significant mass and complexity, which conflicts with Starship\\u2019s design goals.\\n  - Feasibility: Theoretically sound but impractical with current technology. The energy and weight penalties make this unfeasible for a reusable spacecraft like Starship.\\nc. Foldable Ship (Post 1927307395157385351 by MichelVandegaer)\\nSuggestion: Make Starship foldable, like a butterfly folding into a cocoon, to reduce its size during reentry and protect it with a smaller heat shield.\\nAnalysis:\\n  - Pros: Reducing the surface area during reentry lowers the heat load and the area needing protection, potentially simplifying the heat shield design.\\n  - Cons:\\n    - Structural Integrity: Folding a 50-meter-tall, 9-meter-wide spacecraft made of stainless steel, with a complex internal structure (fuel tanks, engines, payload bay), is a monumental engineering challenge. The hinges, actuators, and mechanisms would add weight and failure points.\\n    - Reentry Dynamics: A smaller, folded shape might increase the ballistic coefficient, leading to a sharper, more intense heat pulse during reentry, potentially overwhelming the smaller heat shield.\\n    - Payload Delivery: Folding after payload delivery (e.g., on Mars) assumes the payload can be deployed without the ship\\u2019s full structure, which may not be practical for large cargo or habitats.\\n  - Feasibility: Intriguing but highly impractical. The added complexity and risk outweigh the benefits, especially given Starship\\u2019s already optimized reentry profile.\\n--\\nWhy Hexagonal Tiles? A Deeper Look\\nSpaceX\\u2019s choice of hexagonal tiles, as seen in the image from Thread 1 (Post 1926820184121053604), isn\\u2019t arbitrary. Let\\u2019s explore why this design persists despite the challenges:\\nTesselation Efficiency: Hexagons tesselate perfectly, covering a surface without gaps (unlike squares, which can create straight-line paths for hot gas). Musk noted in 2019 that this shape offers \\u201cno straight path for hot gas to accelerate through the gaps\\u201d (Space.com, 2019).\\nThermal Performance: The 2019 tests showed hex tiles withstanding 1,377\\u00b0C for a full reentry profile, with some erosion but no catastrophic failure. The flat arrangement minimizes turbulence compared to overlapping designs.\\nManufacturability: Hexagons are easier to mass-produce and install uniformly across Starship\\u2019s large surface area (thousands of tiles are needed). Overlapping designs, as Judge Gibson suggests, would require more complex shaping and alignment, increasing production costs.\\nHowever, the current design isn\\u2019t perfect:\\nTile Loss: Canraptor\\u2019s reply (Post 1926930859791106529) suggests more than 35 tiles were lost or damaged in the May 25, 2025, event, indicating ongoing reliability issues.\\nMaintenance: Like the Shuttle, Starship\\u2019s tiles may require frequent replacement, which Musk is trying to avoid to achieve rapid reusability.\\n--\\nPotential Solutions SpaceX Might Explore\\nGiven Musk\\u2019s acknowledgment that \\u201cit will take many more design iterations\\u201d to perfect the heat shield, here are some directions SpaceX might consider, building on the thread\\u2019s ideas and historical lessons:\\na. Advanced Materials\\nNickel-Chromium Alloys: The Encyclopedia.com result mentions Inconel 617, a nickel-chromium alloy proposed for the X-33 heat shield. Such alloys could be used as a secondary layer beneath ceramic tiles to provide additional thermal protection if tiles are lost.\\nAblative-Ceramic Hybrids: Combine the durability of ceramics with ablative materials that erode sacrificially to absorb heat, reducing the burden on the tiles.\\nb. Active Cooling Systems\\nThe 2019 Space.com article notes SpaceX\\u2019s plan to add cooling systems if erosion persists. This could involve:\\n  - Transpiration Cooling: Injecting a coolant (e.g., cryogenic methane from Starship\\u2019s fuel tanks) through porous tiles to absorb heat and create a protective gas layer.\\n  - Microchannel Cooling: Integrate microchannels into the tiles (not on the surface, as RationalEtienne suggests, to avoid plasma interaction) to circulate coolant internally.\\nc. Tile Attachment and Redundancy\\nImprove tile attachment methods to reduce loss. For example, using stronger adhesives or mechanical fasteners that can withstand thermal expansion and vibration.\\nDesign a \\u201clayered\\u201d heat shield where an underlying layer (e.g., a thin Inconel sheet) provides backup protection if tiles are lost, reducing the need for perfect tile reliability.\\nd. Plasma Flow Management\\nInstead of overlapping tiles, explore surface texturing or subtle ridges that guide plasma flow without creating height differences. This could draw inspiration from shark denticles but on a macro scale, using ceramic-compatible manufacturing techniques.\\n--\\nWhy This Matters: The Bigger Picture\\nThe heat shield challenge is a linchpin for SpaceX\\u2019s vision of making life multiplanetary. A reusable, reliable heat shield enables:\\nCost Reduction: Rapid reusability without extensive maintenance lowers the cost per launch, crucial for Mars colonization.\\nMars Mission Success: Surviving Mars entry with a heavy payload (e.g., 100-150 tons of cargo) is non-negotiable for building a self-sustaining colony.\\nEarth Return: Starship must also survive Earth reentry after returning from Mars, where entry speeds could be even higher (up to 11 km\\/s for a direct return trajectory).\\nMusk\\u2019s engagement in this thread\\u2014explaining the physics, acknowledging feedback, and highlighting the iterative process\\u2014shows his hands-on role as SpaceX\\u2019s chief engineer, as users like Sergiorea note (Post 1927301281552491002). It also underscores the collaborative nature of innovation, with users like Judge Gibson, RationalEtienne, and others contributing ideas, even if they\\u2019re not immediately feasible.\\n--\\nConclusion\\nMusk\\u2019s rejection of overlapping tiles stems from fundamental aerodynamic and thermal challenges: varying angles of attack make consistent airflow alignment impossible, and the plasma \\u201cwaterfall\\u201d effect would erode tiles at height differences. These issues are rooted in the physics of hypersonic reentry and the unique demands of Mars missions, which require a high ballistic coefficient and thus a robust heat shield. While alternative ideas like shark denticles, magnetic fields, and foldable designs are creative, they face significant practical hurdles. SpaceX\\u2019s hexagonal tile design, despite its flaws, remains the most viable approach for now, but Musk\\u2019s admission of \\u201cmany more design iterations\\u201d suggests ongoing innovation\\u2014potentially involving advanced materials, active cooling, or plasma flow management\\u2014to achieve the holy grail of a truly reusable orbital heat shield. This challenge is a microcosm of the broader difficulty of making spaceflight routine, affordable, and text:
I have come to the perhaps obvious conclusion that accelerating GDP growth is essential.

@DOGE has and will do great work to postpone the day of bankruptcy of America, but the profligacy of government means that only radical improvements in productivity can save our country.
embedded_text: @farzyness I wonder if this why Elon has somewhat moved away from politics in order to accelerate GDP growth with humanoid robots. If he believes this is the only solution then this would be his most logical choice.

[{"from_index": 0, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 54}]}, "text": "THE KARDASHEV SCALE: MEASURING A CIVILIZATION'S POWER The Kardashev Scale, proposed by Soviet astronomer Nikolai Kardashev in 1964, ranks a civilization's technological advancement based on its energy consumption: Type I: A civilization that harnesses all energy available on its home planet, including solar, wind, and geothermal. Think global unity and sustainable tech. Earth is still a Type 0, but we're inching closer. Type II: A civilization that controls the entire energy output of its star, possibly via megastructures like Dyson Spheres. This is interstellar-level mastery. Type III: A galactic civilization, tapping the energy of an entire galaxy. Imagine billions of stars and planets working in sync - pure sci-fi! text:
But we need a heat shield that can handle Mars atmospheric entry conditions with a heavy payload, so the ballistic coefficient will necessarily be high. Steel is unfortunately hopeless.

No one has ever made a truly reusable orbital heat shield, so this is an extremely tough problem. [As you know] The Shuttle’s shields required extensive rework between flights.

It will take many more design iterations for SpaceX to make the shield work well. We had to build the entire supply chain from glass and alumina to finished tiles, which is why the SpaceX heat shield factory at the Cape is shockingly enormous.

text: How many real people are still on the Internet?
embedded_text:
AI BOTS RUN THE INTERNET NOW

Congrats, humans: you officially lost the internet to AI-powered bots in 2024.

@Voltalius @levelsio In hearts where kindness blooms, a gentle light,
Compassion weaves its threads through human care.
With tender words, we lift the soul's dark night,
And share the warmth that banishes despair.
Each act of love, a ripple soft and wide,
Does mend the weary, broken, and forlorn.

text: This is what it takes to make life multiplanetary
embedded_text:
This isn’t Apollo. This isn’t The Martian. This is the Normandy Landings in space. 

And it’s the right way to go. A lot of the difficulties of surviving in space can be brute forced by throwing more mass at them. https://t.co/xlWFFKMG0x

text: Government is wasting about $100 million per year on unused phone lines!
embedded_text:
Unused phone lines!

Of the 1048 @USOPM phone lines, 198 (19%) were either unused or duplicated.  
OPM cancelled these lines (took 1 hour), resulting in ~$100k/yr in savings. 

Since OPM is ~0.1% of the federal government, this suggests that ~$100M/yr is wasted on unused lines.
